---
title: "Resampling: differentiable interpolation"
---

The transform ladder of the last two chapters produced a *map* $\varphi$. This chapter is how you
*apply* it - turn $\varphi$ into the warped image $I_m\circ\varphi$ - and, just as importantly, how
gradients flow *back* through that application to the transform. It is where the group action of the
[foundations chapter](foundations.qmd#sec-pullback) becomes a concrete, differentiable computation: the
pull-back $\pi(\varphi): I_m\mapsto I_m\circ\varphi$ is the transform group's representation on images,
and resampling is the gather that evaluates it. One idea organises everything.

- **Resampling is "sample an image at a set of continuous coordinates."** Two questions are *orthogonal*:
  *where* the coordinates come from (an affine grid, a deformation field, or an align-corners resize),
  and *how* neighbouring voxels are weighted into each value (the *kernel*). In `nitrix`,
  `geometry/grid.py` owns the coordinate source (`identity_grid`, `spatial_transform`, `resample`);
  `geometry/_interpolate.py` owns the kernel (an ADT of `Interpolator` records - `Linear`,
  `NearestNeighbour`, `Lanczos`, `CubicBSpline`, `MultiLabel`). Adding a kernel never touches the grid
  code.
- **Differentiability is a property of the kernel, in two slots.** Each kernel declares
  `differentiable_in_values` and `differentiable_in_coords`. The second is the one a registration loss
  needs - a smooth-weight kernel (`Linear`, `Lanczos`, `CubicBSpline`) lets the loss push on $\theta$; a
  piecewise-constant one (`NearestNeighbour`) or a hard label selection (`MultiLabel`) hands the optimiser
  a zero coordinate-gradient.

In one sentence: a transform becomes an image by gathering, the gather is the group acting on the image,
and that gather is differentiable in the transform exactly when its weights move smoothly with the
coordinates.

The reader arrives here with two things in hand. From the [foundations chapter](foundations.qmd#sec-pullback)
you know the *pull-back convention*: we map $\varphi:\Omega_f\to\Omega_m$ and the warped moving image is
the *composition* $I_m\circ\varphi$ - for each fixed-grid voxel $x$ we look up where it came from in the
moving image and read that intensity. From the [transforms chapter](transforms.qmd#sec-fit) you know how
a rigid or affine $\varphi$ is parametrised and how it acts on coordinates. What neither chapter said is
the part that makes $I_m\circ\varphi$ a *number*: $\varphi(x)$ is almost never an integer voxel, so "read
that intensity" requires *interpolation*. That is this chapter. We own the step from a coordinate map to
an image, and the gradient that runs the other way.

## A motivating problem {#sec-motivation}

Hold the running task from foundations: you have aligned a subject's T1 to MNI and the optimiser has
converged on a transform $\varphi$. You now need two concrete things.

1. **Produce the aligned image.** Apply $\varphi$ to the subject T1 to get the warped volume that
   actually sits in MNI space - the artefact you save to disk, feed to an atlas, or overlay on the
   template. This is one forward call.
2. **Differentiate it.** During the *fitting* that produced $\varphi$, every optimiser step needed the
   gradient of the mismatch energy with respect to the transform parameters $\theta$. That energy is
   computed on $I_m\circ\varphi$, so the gradient has to flow *through the resampling* - from the loss,
   back through the interpolation weights, into $\varphi(x)$, and finally into $\theta$. If the resampler
   is not differentiable in its coordinates, the whole chain is dead and no gradient-based optimiser can
   move.

Both are the same operation run two ways: forward to make a picture, reverse to make a gradient. The job
of this chapter is to make that operation exact, fast, and differentiable - and to be honest about the
one kernel (nearest neighbour) and the one routine (multi-label) where the coordinate gradient is
*deliberately* zero.

## Pull-back sampling: where the coordinates come from {#sec-pullback-sampling}

### The identity grid, and absolute vs relative coordinates

Begin with the simplest possible map: the one that does nothing. If the warped image is "for each output
voxel, look up a coordinate in the input," then the *no-op* must be "for each output voxel $x$, look up
coordinate $x$." That array - each cell holding its own index - is the *identity grid*, and it is the
seed for everything dense.

`identity_grid(spatial_shape)` builds it. For a 2-D image of shape $(H, W)$ it returns a $(H, W, 2)$
array with `grid[i, j] == (i, j)`; for 3-D $(D, H, W)$ a $(D, H, W, 3)$ array with
`grid[k, i, j] == (k, i, j)`. The layout is the unit's convention throughout: *channels-last*, with the
trailing axis of length `ndim` holding the coordinate, and multi-axis meshes built with `indexing='ij'`
so axis order matches array order. **HOST-CTOR** (it materialises a fixed grid from a static shape; build
it once, outside any jitted loop).

This sets up the unit's coordinate convention precisely. There are two ways to write a deformation, and
confusing them is the most common resampling bug:

- A *displacement field* $u(x)$ is *relative* - it stores the *offset* from each voxel, so the map is
  $\varphi(x) = x + u(x)$. A learned network or a velocity integration produces $u$.
- A *deformation / sampling grid* is *absolute* - it stores the *coordinate to sample at* directly. This
  is what the sampler actually consumes.

The bridge between them is one addition:

$$
\text{deformation} \;=\; \texttt{identity\_grid}(\text{spatial}) \;+\; u
\;=\; x + u(x).
$$ {#eq-id-plus-u}

So `identity_grid` is not decoration: it is the operator that converts the *relative* thing an optimiser
or a network naturally produces into the *absolute* thing the sampler reads. Add zero and you sample the
input back exactly (the identity warp - the identity element of the group acting trivially); add $u$ and
you sample at $x + u(x)$.

### `spatial_transform`: warp by arbitrary coordinates

`spatial_transform(image, deformation, *, mode, cval, method)` is the general warp. For each output
position $p$ it returns `image` sampled at `deformation[p]` via the kernel `method`. The key word is
*absolute*: `deformation` is the coordinate map, not a displacement - to warp by a displacement $u$ you
pass `identity_grid(spatial) + u` per @eq-id-plus-u (the docstring spells this out). This is the
operation that realises the pull-back $I_m\circ\varphi$:

$$
(I_m\circ\varphi)(x) \;=\; \texttt{sample}\big(I_m,\ \varphi(x)\big),
\qquad \varphi(x) = \texttt{deformation}[x].
$$ {#eq-pullback-sample}

This is the group's representation made concrete: $\texttt{sample}(\cdot, \varphi(x))$ is *linear* in the
image (a weighted gather), so $\pi(\varphi)$ is a linear operator on the space of images, and composing
two warps composes their gathers - the representation property of [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-pullback).
The signature carries the unit's shape discipline. Images are $(\ast\text{leading}, \ast\text{spatial},
c)$ - leading batch dims, then `ndim` spatial axes, then a trailing channel axis. Deformations are
$(\ast\text{leading}, \ast\text{spatial}, \text{ndim})$ - the same leading and spatial axes, then a
trailing coordinate axis of length `ndim`. Leading batch dims must *match exactly*; `spatial_transform`
deliberately does *not* broadcast a shared image or deformation, because the trailing-axis semantics
differ between the two operands and a silent broadcast there is too error-prone. Internally the per-sample
core (taking $(\ast\text{spatial}, c)$ and $(\ast\text{spatial}, \text{ndim})$) is wrapped in one
`jax.vmap` per leading axis. **DIFF-JAX** (jittable and differentiable, given a differentiable kernel).

When the batch axis sits on only *one* operand - a batch of images under a single shared warp, or one
image under a batch of warps - the exact-match rule forces an explicit `jax.vmap` with the right
`in_axes`. `spatial_transform_batched` is the convenience that picks `in_axes` for you: it maps over *one*
leading axis from whichever operand carries it, and raises if neither does (call `spatial_transform`
directly for the unbatched case). To broadcast a shared operand across *several* leading dims, broadcast
it to the full batch shape and call `spatial_transform` (which composes multiple matching leading dims
natively). This wrapper is small but real - it shipped to save every consuming model one `vmap` line and
keep the cross-model surface uniform. **DIFF-JAX**.

Two relatives live alongside the grid warp. `sample_at_points(volume, points)` is the *scattered-point*
complement: instead of a coordinate *grid* it takes an arbitrary list of points $(\ast n, \text{ndim})$
in index space and interpolates the volume at each (handling both channel-free $(\ast\text{spatial})$ and
channels-last $(\ast\text{spatial}, c)$ volumes). And `integrate_velocity_field` - the diffeomorphic
exponential, covered on [the diffeomorphic chapter](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf) - is built *on*
`spatial_transform`: scaling-and-squaring composes a field with itself by warping it through
`identity_grid + phi` each doubling step. Resampling is the substrate the whole velocity-field machinery
stands on. **DIFF-JAX**.

### `resample`: the align-corners resize grid

`resample(image, target_shape, *, mode, cval, method)` is the *other* coordinate source: an axis-aligned
*resize*. Same sampler, same kernels - only the coordinates differ. Where do the resize coordinates come
from? We want the output to keep the input's two endpoints pinned (so the corners line up) and spread the
interior evenly. For an axis of input size $N$ resampled to $M$ points, output pixel $i$ samples the
input at

$$
c_i \;=\; i\cdot\frac{N-1}{M-1},
\qquad i = 0,\dots,M-1.
$$ {#eq-align-corners}

At $i=0$ this is coordinate $0$ (the first input pixel) and at $i=M-1$ it is $N-1$ (the last) - the
endpoints are exact. This is scipy's `align_corners=True` convention; `resample` builds it with
`jnp.linspace(0, N-1, M)` per axis (and the degenerate $M=1$ case samples coordinate $0$). The grid is
the *outer product* of those per-axis vectors, which matters for speed (@sec-separable). **DIFF-JAX**.

So the two grid functions split cleanly. `spatial_transform` takes coordinates *from a deformation field*
(an affine grid, a learned displacement, an integrated velocity); `resample` takes coordinates *from a
resize rule*. They call one sampler.

### The unit spine: kernel ⟂ coordinate source

This is the structural idea to carry through the chapter, and it is worth stating once in full. A
resampling operation is the composition of two independent choices:

| axis | question | owner |
|---|---|---|
| **coordinate source** | *where* do we sample? | `geometry/grid.py` (`spatial_transform`, `resample`, `identity_grid`) |
| **kernel** | *how* are voxels weighted into each value? | `geometry/_interpolate.py` (the `Interpolator` records) |

Neither axis knows about the other. You can resize with `Lanczos` or warp by a deformation with
`Lanczos`; you can resize with `Linear` or warp with `Linear`. The kernel is passed as `method=` and the
grid functions never branch on which one it is (they dispatch through one shared seam,
`_sample_at_coords`). This is the same forward-*method* ⟂ backward-*format* factoring the linear-algebra
unit uses for its eigensolver: the *task* (coordinate source) is orthogonal to the *method* (kernel), and
- once a Pallas gather lands - to the *backend* (execution engine). Adding a kernel is adding one frozen
record; the grid functions are untouched.

## The kernels: how voxels are weighted {#sec-kernels}

### The `Interpolator` protocol

A kernel is "anything that can sample an image at continuous coordinates with a fixed rule." `nitrix`
encodes that as a `Protocol` - structural conformance, not inheritance - so each concrete kernel stays a
plain frozen, hashable dataclass with no array leaves. The protocol asks for one method and two flags:

```python
def sample(self, image, coords, *, mode, cval) -> sampled    # (*out, c)
differentiable_in_values: ClassVar[bool]
differentiable_in_coords: ClassVar[bool]
```

`sample` maps an unbatched channels-last image $(\ast\text{spatial}, c)$ and a coordinate field
$(\ast\text{out}, \text{ndim})$ to sampled values $(\ast\text{out}, c)$, where `coords[..., d]` is the
position along input axis $d$. The two flags are the heart of this chapter and we treat them on their own
below. Because a kernel's configuration (a spline order, a static label set) is *structural* rather than
a differentiable array, the records are *not* registered as pytrees: they ride `jit` static arguments and
`custom_vjp` non-differentiable slots directly (the `SolverSpec` pattern from the linalg unit).

The records are re-exported from `geometry.grid`, so the public surface is `Linear`, `NearestNeighbour`,
`Lanczos`, `CubicBSpline`, `MultiLabel`.

### `Linear` — the default

Each sampled value is the multilinear blend of the $2^{\text{ndim}}$ surrounding voxels. In 1-D: let
$b=\lfloor c\rfloor$ and $f = c - b$; the two taps are $\{b, b+1\}$ with weights $\{1-f,\ f\}$. The
weights sum to 1 (a partition of unity, so constants are preserved) and move *smoothly* with $c$. That
smoothness is exactly why `Linear` is the default for differentiable registration: the sampled value is
$C^0$ in the coordinate, with a well-defined gradient $\partial/\partial c = \text{(upper voxel)} -
\text{(lower voxel)}$ almost everywhere. `differentiable_in_values = True`,
`differentiable_in_coords = True`.

### `NearestNeighbour` — exact, but coord-flat

Each sampled value is the single voxel nearest the sample position, with rounding $\lfloor c +
0.5\rfloor$ (round half *up*, matching `map_coordinates(order=0)` - not round-half-to-even). One tap,
weight 1. Because the output is restricted to values that already occur in the input, this is the
label-preserving choice when you do *not* want anti-aliasing.

But the round is *piecewise-constant* in $c$: nudge the coordinate and the sampled value does not change
until you cross a half-integer, where it jumps. So the gradient with respect to the *coordinate* is zero
almost everywhere and undefined on a measure-zero set - useless to an optimiser. Hence
`differentiable_in_coords = False`. The gather is still a one-hot pick of an image value, so
`differentiable_in_values = True`. The practical upshot, and the reason the flag exists: *you cannot
register with `NearestNeighbour`.* Use it to warp a label map *after* you have found $\varphi$ with a
smooth kernel; never as the kernel inside the loss.

### `Lanczos(order)` — windowed sinc

The high-fidelity differentiable kernel. The ideal band-limited interpolator is the sinc, but sinc has
infinite support; Lanczos windows it with a second, wider sinc to get a finite stencil. The kernel of
radius $a=\texttt{order}$ is

$$
L_a(x) \;=\; \operatorname{sinc}(x)\,\operatorname{sinc}(x/a),
\qquad |x| < a,
$$ {#eq-lanczos}

and zero outside (here $\operatorname{sinc}$ is the *normalised* sinc, $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$, equal to 1
at 0 - so $L_a$ is 1 at the sample and 0 at every other integer offset, reproducing grid samples
exactly). The $2a$ taps per axis run $\lfloor c\rfloor - a + 1 \dots \lfloor c\rfloor + a$. Raw Lanczos
weights sum to *approximately* 1 but not exactly, so `nitrix` *renormalises* them per axis to sum to 1 -
making the separable outer product a true partition of unity and preserving constants exactly. The
weights are smooth in $x$, so the kernel is differentiable end-to-end in both values and coordinates: it
is the high-quality alternative to `Linear` for registration and anti-aliased resampling. `order`
defaults to 3 (the common ANTs/ITK choice); larger is sharper and wider but more ringing-prone. This is
the ANTs `LanczosWindowedSinc` *algorithm class*, not bit-exact ITK (radius/normalisation conventions
differ in detail). `order` must be a positive integer (validated in `__post_init__`).

### `CubicBSpline` — the order-3 interpolating spline

The path that nnUNet / `hd_bet`-style preprocessing expects (`scipy.ndimage` `order=3`). The subtlety: a
cubic B-spline interpolator is *not* a plain cubic convolution. The cubic B-spline *basis* $\beta^3$ is
smooth and a partition of unity, but applied directly to the samples it only *approximates* them - it
blurs. The interpolation property (the curve passes *through* the samples) is recovered by a two-step
operation:

1. a *prefilter* converts the image samples into interpolating B-spline *coefficients*, and
2. a 4-tap separable *cubic basis* gathers those coefficients.

The prefilter is the interesting part. The cubic B-spline has a single real pole

$$
z_1 \;=\; \sqrt{3} - 2 \;\approx\; -0.2679,
$$ {#eq-bspline-pole}

and converting samples to coefficients is the *recursive deconvolution* by that pole - the Unser /
Aldroubi / Eden algorithm. Concretely: scale by the gain $(1-z_1)(1-1/z_1) = 6$, run a *causal*
first-order recurrence $c_k = s_k + z_1 c_{k-1}$ forward, then an *anti-causal* recurrence backward, once
per spatial axis (the channel axis is left alone). The boundary is handled by the closed-form
*whole-sample-mirror* initial coefficient (the Thévenaz / ITK convention) - computed as a single
vectorised dot product, no scan, so it stays parallel and differentiable. With the prefilter in place the
result passes through the samples exactly; the basis itself is $C^2$-smooth, so the kernel is
differentiable in both the values (prefilter and gather are linear) and the coordinates (the basis is
smooth).

Two engineering notes worth carrying. First, the recurrence is run two ways behind one interface -
sequentially with `lax.scan` on CPU, and in parallel with `lax.associative_scan` ($O(\log n)$ depth) on
GPU - because a first-order linear recurrence composes associatively. This is the same platform split
`signal._iir` uses, and both forms are exact (they agree to a ULP). An FFT convolution (the *other*
`_iir` engine) is deliberately *not* used here: the cubic pole is mild, so the prefilter's impulse
response is a short (~25-tap) FIR, too short for the transform overhead to pay off. Second: the result is
*bit-exact* with `scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates(order=3, mode='mirror')`, interior and boundary
(~$10^{-15}$) - the genuine parity the design doc set out to ship (this kernel was the resolved request
`docs/feature-requests/cubic-resample.md`). There is one boundary caveat, important enough to get its own
section (@sec-boundary). `differentiable_in_values = True`, `differentiable_in_coords = True`.

### `MultiLabel(labels, inner)` — resampling a segmentation

Resampling a *label* map is its own problem. Nearest-neighbour aliases label boundaries and drops thin
structures; plain `Linear` invents non-label values *between* labels (halfway between label 2 and label 5
is not label 3.5 - it is nonsense). `MultiLabel` is the ANTs-style cure and it does neither. The idea:
treat each label as a binary indicator mask, resample *that* smoothly, and take the winner.

$$
\text{out}(x) \;=\; \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\ell\,\in\,\texttt{labels}}\
\big(\text{inner.sample}(\mathbb{1}[I = \ell])\big)(x).
$$ {#eq-multilabel}

Each label's indicator is resampled with the `inner` kernel (default `Linear`); the per-voxel *arg-max*
over those resampled scores is the output label. Boundaries are anti-aliased (the inner kernel smooths
each mask) yet the output is restricted to the input label set - no invented values. The implementation
is a memory-bounded *running arg-max* fold over the label set: $O(K)$ inner resamples carrying just two
output-shaped arrays (best score, best label), never a $K$-channel stack.

Two contract details. The label set is a *static* constructor tuple, not discovered at runtime:
`jnp.unique` is dynamically shaped and would break `jit`, so you pass `labels=tuple(...)` (contiguous
labels are `tuple(range(K))`). *Earlier labels win ties*, and a sample with no label support (e.g. fully
out of bounds under `mode='constant'`) resolves to `labels[0]` - so *list the background label first*.
The `inner` kernel's *support width* sets how much anti-aliasing happens: `Linear` (2-tap) is a cheap
sub-voxel area weighting that refines nearest neighbour at junctions but, being narrow, still drops
sub-pixel structure under heavy downsampling (it coincides with `NN` on a 2× decimation); a wider inner -
`Lanczos` - area-weights over a larger neighbourhood and preserves thin structures (the stronger
anti-aliasing ANTs `MultiLabel`, a Gaussian, is known for), at higher cost. The arg-max is a hard
selection, so `MultiLabel` is *non-differentiable* in both values and coordinates (`grad` returns zeros,
it does not error); `cval` is ignored. **DIFF-JAX** in execution class (jittable) but with a zero
gradient - fine for warping a final segmentation, never inside a loss.

### What the two flags mean for backprop

Pulling the thread together, here is the differentiability table the loss cares about:

| kernel | `…_in_values` | `…_in_coords` | usable in a registration loss? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Linear` | ✓ | ✓ | yes (the default) |
| `Lanczos` | ✓ | ✓ | yes (higher fidelity) |
| `CubicBSpline` | ✓ | ✓ | yes (order-3, scipy parity) |
| `NearestNeighbour` | ✓ | ✗ | **no** (coord-flat) |
| `MultiLabel` | ✗ | ✗ | **no** (hard arg-max) |

`differentiable_in_values` is whether the output has a gradient with respect to the input *intensities* -
relevant if you are optimising the image (a generative model, an atlas). `differentiable_in_coords` is
whether it has a gradient with respect to the *sample coordinates* - and since the loss reaches $\theta$
only through $\varphi(x)$, this is the flag a coordinate-driven registration needs. The chain is

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial \theta}
=\sum_x \frac{\partial \mathcal{S}}{\partial (I_m\circ\varphi)(x)}\;
\underbrace{\frac{\partial\, \texttt{sample}(I_m, \varphi(x))}{\partial \varphi(x)}}_{\text{kernel: needs } \texttt{diff\_in\_coords}}\;
\frac{\partial \varphi(x)}{\partial \theta}.
$$ {#eq-chain}

If the middle factor is zero - `NearestNeighbour`, `MultiLabel` - the sum is zero and the optimiser
cannot move, no matter how good the metric or the transform parametrisation. That is the single most
important consequence of this chapter for anyone building a registration: *the kernel you fit with must
be smooth in its coordinates.* Fit with `Linear` (or `Lanczos`/`CubicBSpline`); apply the result to a
label map with `MultiLabel` or `NearestNeighbour`.

@fig-resampling-kernels shows the kernels side by side and what they do to a hard edge.

```{python}
#| label: fig-resampling-kernels
#| fig-cap: "Left: the interpolation kernels as a function of the offset from a sample. `Linear` is the 2-tap triangle; the cubic B-spline basis is the 4-tap bell; `Lanczos(3)` is the 6-tap windowed sinc — note its *negative side lobes*, the source of ringing. Right: each kernel resampling a step+sine 1-D signal (dots = samples) near the discontinuity. `Linear` (blue) rounds the corner most — it blurs; `Lanczos` (green) overshoots on both sides of the step — it rings; the cubic B-spline sits between. This is the classic ringing-vs-blur trade the kernel choice buys. (The 1-D B-spline panel applies the basis directly to illustrate the curve shape; the real `CubicBSpline` prefilters first so it interpolates the samples exactly.)"
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def linear_kernel(x):
    return np.where(np.abs(x) < 1.0, 1.0 - np.abs(x), 0.0)

def cubic_bspline_kernel(x):
    ax = np.abs(x)
    w = np.zeros_like(ax)
    m1 = ax < 1.0
    m2 = (ax >= 1.0) & (ax < 2.0)
    w[m1] = 2.0 / 3.0 - ax[m1] ** 2 + 0.5 * ax[m1] ** 3
    w[m2] = ((2.0 - ax[m2]) ** 3) / 6.0
    return w

def lanczos_kernel(x, a=3):
    k = np.sinc(x) * np.sinc(x / a)   # numpy sinc is sin(pi t)/(pi t)
    return np.where(np.abs(x) < a, k, 0.0)

xs = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 1001)

N = 24
grid = np.arange(N)
signal = np.where(grid < N // 2, 0.2, 0.8) + 0.12 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * grid / 6.0)
fine = np.linspace(0, N - 1, 600)

def mirror_index(i, n):
    if n == 1:
        return np.zeros_like(i)
    period = 2 * (n - 1)
    f = np.mod(i, period)
    return np.where(f >= n, period - f, f)

def resample_1d(sig, coords, kernel, support):
    out = np.zeros_like(coords)
    base = np.floor(coords).astype(int)
    for off in range(-support + 1, support + 1):
        taps = base + off
        idx = mirror_index(taps, len(sig))
        out += kernel(coords - taps) * sig[idx]
    return out

def resample_lanczos(sig, coords, a=3):
    out = np.zeros_like(coords)
    wsum = np.zeros_like(coords)
    base = np.floor(coords).astype(int)
    for off in range(-a + 1, a + 1):
        taps = base + off
        idx = mirror_index(taps, len(sig))
        w = lanczos_kernel(coords - taps, a)
        out += w * sig[idx]
        wsum += w
    return out / wsum

y_lin = resample_1d(signal, fine, linear_kernel, support=1)
y_cub = resample_1d(signal, fine, cubic_bspline_kernel, support=2)
y_lan = resample_lanczos(signal, fine, a=3)

fig, (axk, axr) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.2))
axk.plot(xs, linear_kernel(xs), label="Linear (2 taps)", lw=2)
axk.plot(xs, cubic_bspline_kernel(xs), label="cubic B-spline basis (4 taps)", lw=2)
axk.plot(xs, lanczos_kernel(xs, 3), label="Lanczos a=3 (6 taps)", lw=2)
axk.axhline(0.0, color="0.7", lw=0.8)
for t in range(-3, 4):
    axk.axvline(t, color="0.9", lw=0.6)
axk.set_title("Interpolation kernels: weight vs offset")
axk.set_xlabel("offset $x$ from a sample (voxels)")
axk.set_ylabel("weight $K(x)$")
axk.legend(fontsize=8)

axr.plot(grid, signal, "o", color="0.3", ms=4, label="samples")
axr.plot(fine, y_lin, lw=1.6, label="Linear (blur at the step)")
axr.plot(fine, y_cub, lw=1.6, label="cubic B-spline")
axr.plot(fine, y_lan, lw=1.6, label="Lanczos a=3 (ringing)")
axr.set_xlim(N // 2 - 5, N // 2 + 5)
axr.set_title("Resampling a step+sine: ringing vs blur")
axr.set_xlabel("position (voxels)")
axr.set_ylabel("intensity")
axr.legend(fontsize=8)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

And @fig-resampling-warp is the chapter's motivating artefact: a transform applied to an image to produce
the warped result, the forward direction of @eq-pullback-sample.

```{python}
#| label: fig-resampling-warp
#| fig-cap: "Applying a transform is a pull-back. Left: a checkerboard phantom with a smooth analytic displacement field $u$ overlaid as a quiver (a decaying swirl about the centre). Right: the warped image, obtained by sampling the phantom at `identity_grid + u` with bilinear interpolation and edge-clamp boundary — exactly what `spatial_transform(image, identity_grid(spatial) + u, method=Linear(), mode='nearest')` computes. The straight checker lines bend along the flow; this single gather, differentiated, is what carries a registration loss back to the transform."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

H = W = 160
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:H, 0:W].astype(float)
checker = (((xx // 16).astype(int) + (yy // 16).astype(int)) % 2).astype(float)

cy, cx = H / 2, W / 2
rx = (xx - cx) / W
ry = (yy - cy) / H
r2 = rx ** 2 + ry ** 2
amp = 22.0
decay = np.exp(-r2 / 0.08)
u_row = amp * decay * (-rx)
u_col = amp * decay * (ry)
u = np.stack([u_row, u_col], axis=-1)

grid = np.stack(
    np.meshgrid(np.arange(H), np.arange(W), indexing="ij"), axis=-1
).astype(float)
defm = grid + u   # identity_grid + u : absolute sampling coordinates

def sample_bilinear(img, coords):
    cr, cc = coords[..., 0], coords[..., 1]
    r0 = np.floor(cr).astype(int)
    c0 = np.floor(cc).astype(int)
    fr, fc = cr - r0, cc - c0
    r0c, r1c = np.clip(r0, 0, H - 1), np.clip(r0 + 1, 0, H - 1)
    c0c, c1c = np.clip(c0, 0, W - 1), np.clip(c0 + 1, 0, W - 1)
    top = img[r0c, c0c] * (1 - fc) + img[r0c, c1c] * fc
    bot = img[r1c, c0c] * (1 - fc) + img[r1c, c1c] * fc
    return top * (1 - fr) + bot * fr

warped = sample_bilinear(checker, defm)

fig, (a0, a1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10.5, 5.2))
a0.imshow(checker, cmap="gray", origin="upper")
s = 12
a0.quiver(
    xx[::s, ::s], yy[::s, ::s],
    u[..., 1][::s, ::s], -u[..., 0][::s, ::s],
    color="C1", angles="xy", scale_units="xy", scale=1.0, width=0.004,
)
a0.set_title("moving image + displacement field $u$")
a0.set_xticks([]); a0.set_yticks([])
a1.imshow(warped, cmap="gray", origin="upper")
a1.set_title(r"warped $= I_m(\,\mathrm{id}+u\,)$ (pull-back)")
a1.set_xticks([]); a1.set_yticks([])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Boundary handling: what happens at the edge {#sec-boundary}

A sample coordinate near the edge asks for voxels that do not exist - taps at index $-1$ or $N$. Every
kernel inherits one boundary policy, applied *once*, at the moment integer taps are folded to legal gather
indices (`_boundary_index`). Boundary is a property of the *call*, not the kernel, so it stays a keyword
(`mode` / `cval`) on `sample` / `resample` / `spatial_transform` rather than a field on the record. There
are five modes, matching `jax.scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates` exactly at the integer-tap level. For an axis
of length $n$ and an out-of-range tap $t$:

| mode | rule | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `constant` | tap clamped for the gather, but masked out and replaced by `cval` | fill outside with a constant |
| `nearest` | $\operatorname{clip}(t, 0, n-1)$ | edge-replicate (clamp) |
| `wrap` | $t \bmod n$ | periodic, period $n$ |
| `mirror` | fold with period $2(n-1)$, **no** edge repeat | reflect $\dots c\,b\,a\,b\,c\dots$ |
| `reflect` | fold with period $2n$, **edge repeat** | reflect $\dots b\,a\,a\,b\dots$ |

`constant` is the only mode that uses `cval`: an originally-out-of-range tap is marked invalid and its
contribution replaced by `cval` (the clamp is just so the gather itself never reads out of bounds). The
other four always produce a legal in-range neighbour, so their validity mask is all-ones and `cval` is
ignored. The distinction between `mirror` (period $2(n-1)$) and `reflect` (period $2n$) is whether the
edge sample is repeated at the fold - a one-cell difference that nonetheless changes which neighbour a
boundary tap lands on.

The defaults are chosen per call site for a reason. `spatial_transform` and `resample` default to
`constant` (with `cval=0`) - the safe "outside the image is black" choice for image sampling. But
`integrate_velocity_field` defaults to `nearest` (edge-replicate), because that is the voxelmorph
convention and the *only* mode that yields a numerically sensible integrated flow at the boundary;
`constant` there silently miscomputes the flow within ~`n_steps` voxels of every edge. The lesson
generalises: when you warp a *flow field* (as opposed to an *image*) the right boundary is usually
`nearest`, because a displacement does not "end" at the edge the way intensity does.

### The `CubicBSpline` caveat

`CubicBSpline` is the one kernel that does *not* honour an arbitrary `mode`. Its prefilter only implements
the mirror (whole-sample-symmetric) boundary initialisation, and a B-spline is only self-consistent - the
interpolation property, reproducing the samples, only holds - when the *prefilter and the gather share a
boundary*. So `CubicBSpline` *always* uses mirror for both and ignores the `mode` / `cval` you pass. Per
the suite's "loud fallbacks" tenet, that override is *announced, not silent*: supplying an explicit
non-mirror `mode` (or a non-zero `cval`) raises a `CubicBSplineBoundaryWarning`. The bare default
`mode='constant', cval=0` is treated as *unspecified* and stays silent (so you are not warned for simply
not passing a mode). Pass `mode='mirror'` to silence it deliberately. A mode-aware prefilter (matching
scipy for `nearest`/`reflect`/…) is a planned extension; today, mirror parity is the contract, and that
is exactly the mode scipy's order-3 default uses.

@fig-resampling-boundary shows the five folds on a short 1-D signal.

```{python}
#| label: fig-resampling-boundary
#| fig-cap: "The five boundary modes, shown as how an integer tap outside $[0, n-1]$ is folded back to a legal index on a length-6 signal (grey band = in range, red squares = the extension each mode invents). `constant` fills the outside with `cval` (here 0); `nearest` clamps to the edge value; `wrap` is periodic; `mirror` reflects without repeating the edge cell (period $2(n-1)$); `reflect` reflects *with* an edge repeat (period $2n$). These folds match `jax.scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates` tap-for-tap, and a single `_boundary_index` applies them for every kernel."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = 6
sig = np.array([0.2, 0.5, 0.9, 0.7, 0.4, 0.6])
idx_in = np.arange(n)
taps = np.arange(-4, n + 4)

def fold(taps, size, mode):
    if mode == "nearest":
        return np.clip(taps, 0, size - 1), np.ones_like(taps, bool)
    if mode == "wrap":
        return np.mod(taps, size), np.ones_like(taps, bool)
    if mode == "mirror":
        period = 2 * (size - 1)
        f = np.mod(taps, period)
        return np.where(f >= size, period - f, f), np.ones_like(taps, bool)
    if mode == "reflect":
        period = 2 * size
        f = np.mod(taps, period)
        return np.where(f >= size, period - 1 - f, f), np.ones_like(taps, bool)
    if mode == "constant":
        valid = (taps >= 0) & (taps < size)
        return np.clip(taps, 0, size - 1), valid
    raise ValueError(mode)

modes = ["constant", "nearest", "wrap", "mirror", "reflect"]
cval = 0.0
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(modes), figsize=(13, 3.0), sharey=True)
for ax, mode in zip(axes, modes):
    idx, valid = fold(taps, n, mode)
    vals = sig[idx].copy()
    if mode == "constant":
        vals = np.where(valid, vals, cval)
    ax.axvspan(-0.5, n - 0.5, color="0.92")
    ext = (taps < 0) | (taps >= n)
    ax.plot(idx_in, sig, "o-", color="0.2", ms=5, label="signal")
    ax.plot(taps[ext], vals[ext], "s", color="C3", ms=5, label="extension")
    ax.plot(taps[~ext], vals[~ext], "o", color="0.2", ms=4)
    ax.set_title(mode, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("index (tap)")
    ax.set_xlim(-4.5, n + 3.5)
axes[0].set_ylabel("value")
axes[0].legend(fontsize=7, loc="upper left")
fig.suptitle(
    "The five boundary modes: folding taps outside [0, n-1] (grey = in range)",
    fontsize=11,
)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.93))
plt.show()
```

## Two gather engines, and the separable resize {#sec-separable}

Under the kernels sit two gather *engines*. Which one runs is an internal perf choice; the numerics are
identical to a ULP either way, so the chapter treats it as a performance story, not a correctness one.

**`_map_coordinates_sample` (Linear / NearestNeighbour).** For the orders scipy's `map_coordinates`
supports natively (0 nearest, 1 linear), the kernel lowers onto `jax.scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates`,
mapped over channels with `jax.vmap`. This is the XLA-native gather and, re-measured on the pinned JAX
`cuda12==0.10.1`, it is *faster than the explicit corner gather on the GPU too* - 1.1–1.5× for any image
$\ge 48^3$, across coherent and random coordinates and 1 or 3 channels (the explicit gather only ties it,
in noise, on tiny $\le 64^3$ single-channel volumes). An earlier "GPU prefers the explicit gather"
dispatch was stale; newer XLA tightened the lowering. So `_map_coordinates_sample` is both the runtime
engine for Linear/NN *and* the parity *oracle* the tests pin the explicit gather against. (The same
backend is the subject of the CPU/GPU characterisation in
`docs/feature-requests/interpolation-backend-cpu-gpu-gap.md`: it is GPU-strong when fused into a larger
graph, but XLA-CPU's per-call overhead loses ~5–9× to scipy's tight C loop on the iterated small-grid
`integrate_velocity_field` - a narrow, known gap, not a correctness issue.)

**`_separable_gather` (Lanczos, CubicBSpline).** Lanczos has no `map_coordinates` equivalent, so it uses
the explicit separable gather - and so does the cubic B-spline basis. Each of the `ndim` axes contributes
$T$ integer taps and per-tap weights; the boundary mode folds the taps; the $T^{\text{ndim}}$ corner
values are gathered and contracted against the *outer product* of the per-axis weights. This is the
pure-JAX "explicit 8-corner gather" (in 3-D) and the algorithmic shape a future Pallas pointer-load kernel
ports onto. It is differentiable in the image values (the gather) and - whenever the tap rule makes the
weights depend smoothly on the coordinate - in the coordinates.

### The separable-resize optimisation

For an *arbitrary* warp the corner count is unavoidable: the deformation coordinates are not separable, so
you must evaluate the full $T^{\text{ndim}}$ stencil per output voxel. But a `resample` grid *is*
separable - it is the outer product of the per-axis coordinate vectors of @eq-align-corners. That
structure lets you resample one axis at a time: a single $T$-tap weighted gather along axis 0, then along
axis 1, then axis 2. The cost drops from the dense

$$
O\big(N_{\text{out}}\cdot T^{\text{ndim}}\big)
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
O\big(N_{\text{out}}\cdot T\cdot \text{ndim}\big),
$$ {#eq-separable-cost}

and the two agree to a ULP. The difference is dramatic for a *wide* kernel: `Lanczos(3)` has $T = 2a = 6$
taps, so in 3-D the dense path is $6^3 = 216$ corners per voxel against $6\times3 = 18$ for the 1-D passes
- an order of magnitude. This is `_separable_resample`, selected by the kernel's
`prefers_separable_resample` flag. `Lanczos` and `CubicBSpline` set it `True` (they *need* it to stay
tractable in 3-D); `Linear` and `NearestNeighbour` set it `False`, keeping their resize on the dense
per-coordinate `map_coordinates` path (the 1-D passes are a mild regression on the throughput-sensitive
CPU interpolation path for those low-tap kernels). There is a known curiosity here: because of that flag,
a `Linear` resize currently runs the dense corner gather and can be *slower* than `Lanczos` for the same
resize - counter-intuitive, since linear has the fewest taps - and routing `Linear` resize onto the
separable path is an open optimisation (`docs/feature-requests/linear-resample-separable-path.md`). For
`CubicBSpline` the separable resize first applies the prefilter once to the whole image, then runs the
per-axis passes on the coefficients (forcing mirror, per @sec-boundary). `MultiLabel` is *not* separable
(its output is a per-voxel arg-max, not a separable weighted sum), so a `MultiLabel` resize falls back to
the dense meshgrid gather.

## The Gaussian pyramid: setup for coarse-to-fine {#sec-pyramid}

The transform ladder told you *what* family $\varphi$ lives in; it did not tell you how to avoid the
optimiser falling into a local minimum on a high-resolution image full of fine detail and competing
matches. The standard cure - developed fully on [the optimization chapter](optimization.qmd#sec-coarse-fine)
- is *coarse-to-fine*: solve a blurred, shrunken version of the problem first (few minima, easy basin),
then warm-start each finer resolution from the coarser solution above it. The image stack that makes this
possible is the *Gaussian pyramid*, and `nitrix` builds it by *composition only* - no new kernel, just the
resampling and smoothing you already have.

`gaussian_pyramid(image, *, levels, factor=2.0, sigma=None, mode='reflect', method=Linear())` returns a
tuple of `levels` images, *finest first*: `out[0]` is the full-resolution image and `out[levels-1]` is
the coarsest. (Coarse-to-fine iterates `reversed(out)`.) Each step down is `downsample`. **HOST-CTOR /
DIFF-JAX** (it builds a fixed-length tuple of arrays; jittable once `levels` is fixed).

`downsample(image, *, factor=2.0, sigma=None, mode='reflect', method=Linear())` is two operations in
sequence:

1. an *anti-alias Gaussian* with $\sigma = \texttt{factor}/2$ per axis (the default heuristic: the cutoff
   that suppresses frequencies the coarser grid cannot represent - skip it and you alias high-frequency
   texture into spurious low-frequency structure), then
2. an *align-corners `resample`* to `round(spatial / factor)` (floored at 1 per axis).

`upsample(image, target_shape, *, method=Linear())` is the prolongation: a thin `resample` to a *larger*
shape, with *no anti-alias* (you are not creating new high frequencies going up, so there is nothing to
pre-filter).

### The critical caveat: rescale field values on resize

Here is the trap that bites everyone once. A displacement or velocity *field* stores values that are
*voxel offsets* - a number measured in the grid's own units. When you resize the field, the grid's units
change, so the stored offsets become wrong: an offset of "2 voxels" on a $128$-wide grid is a *different
physical distance* than "2 voxels" on a $64$-wide grid. Resizing the array does *not* fix this. After an
`upsample`/`downsample` of a *field* you must additionally rescale the field *values* per axis by the
size ratio:

$$
u'_{d} \;=\; u_{d}\cdot\frac{\texttt{target}[d]}{\texttt{spatial}[d]}.
$$ {#eq-field-rescale}

This is *semantic*, a property of what the field *means*, not of the resampler - so `upsample`
deliberately resizes only the array and leaves the value rescaling to the caller (the registration driver
applies it explicitly when it lifts a coarse warp to the next pyramid level). For an *image* there is
nothing to rescale (intensities are not lengths); for a *field* @eq-field-rescale is mandatory. Get it
wrong and your coarse-to-fine warp will be off by exactly the pyramid factor at every level - a bug that
looks like "the registration almost works."

@fig-resampling-pyramid shows a synthetic phantom's pyramid; note how the anti-alias suppresses the fine
checker texture in the interior as the grid coarsens (it cannot be represented, so it should not survive).

```{python}
#| label: fig-resampling-pyramid
#| fig-cap: "A four-level Gaussian pyramid of a synthetic phantom (two nested ellipses with a fine checker texture inside the inner one), finest first. Each level is the one above smoothed by a Gaussian of $\\sigma = \\text{factor}/2$ and then resampled to half the size with the align-corners grid. The coarse-to-fine optimiser iterates these in reverse (coarsest first). Note how the high-frequency checker — which the coarse grids cannot represent — is suppressed by the anti-alias smoothing rather than aliased into spurious structure; an image (unlike a displacement field) needs no value rescaling on resize."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

H = W = 192
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:H, 0:W].astype(float)
cy, cx = H / 2, W / 2
phantom = np.zeros((H, W))
e1 = ((xx - cx) / 80) ** 2 + ((yy - cy) / 65) ** 2 <= 1.0
phantom[e1] = 0.4
e2 = ((xx - cx) / 55) ** 2 + ((yy - cy - 5) / 45) ** 2 <= 1.0
phantom[e2] = 0.75
checker = (((xx // 4).astype(int) + (yy // 4).astype(int)) % 2).astype(float)
phantom[e2] += 0.18 * (checker[e2] - 0.5)

def gaussian_blur_separable(img, sigma):
    r = max(1, int(4 * sigma + 0.5))
    t = np.arange(-r, r + 1)
    k = np.exp(-0.5 * (t / sigma) ** 2)
    k /= k.sum()
    out = img
    for axis in (0, 1):
        out = np.apply_along_axis(
            lambda m: np.convolve(np.pad(m, r, mode="reflect"), k, mode="valid"),
            axis, out,
        )
    return out

def resample_align_corners(img, target):
    out = img
    for axis, (in_sz, out_sz) in enumerate(zip(img.shape, target)):
        coords = np.array([0.0]) if out_sz < 2 else np.linspace(0, in_sz - 1, out_sz)
        base = np.floor(coords).astype(int)
        frac = coords - base
        lo = np.clip(base, 0, in_sz - 1)
        hi = np.clip(base + 1, 0, in_sz - 1)
        lo_v = np.take(out, lo, axis=axis)
        hi_v = np.take(out, hi, axis=axis)
        shape = [1] * out.ndim
        shape[axis] = out_sz
        f = frac.reshape(shape)
        out = lo_v * (1 - f) + hi_v * f
    return out

def downsample(img, factor=2.0):
    sm = gaussian_blur_separable(img, sigma=factor / 2.0)
    target = tuple(max(1, int(round(s / factor))) for s in img.shape)
    return resample_align_corners(sm, target)

levels = 4
pyr = [phantom]
cur = phantom
for _ in range(levels - 1):
    cur = downsample(cur, factor=2.0)
    pyr.append(cur)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, levels, figsize=(12, 3.4))
for ax, im, lvl in zip(axes, pyr, range(levels)):
    ax.imshow(im, cmap="gray", vmin=0, vmax=1.0)
    ax.set_title(f"level {lvl}: {im.shape[0]}x{im.shape[1]}")
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle(
    "Gaussian pyramid (finest first): anti-alias sigma=factor/2, then align-corners resample",
    fontsize=11,
)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.94))
plt.show()
```

## The 1-D temporal cousin {#sec-temporal}

Everything above samples a *spatial* grid. There is a one-dimensional sibling in the signal domain worth
knowing about, because it is the same idea - linear interpolation, made differentiable and parallel - on
the time axis. `signal.interpolate.linear_interpolate(data, mask)` fills *censored* frames of an fMRI time
series: given `data` with the observation axis last and a boolean `mask` (`True` = observed, `False` =
missing, e.g. a high-motion frame to drop), it replaces each missing frame with the piecewise-linear
interpolation between the nearest observed frames on either side (leading/trailing gaps are
edge-replicated). The implementation is neat: rather than a sequential scan, it runs *two associative
scans* - a forward prefix-max to find the nearest observed frame at-or-before each position and a reverse
prefix-min for the nearest at-or-after - then interpolates elementwise, giving $O(\log n)$ parallel depth
on GPU (a ~3–5× wall-time win over the legacy sequential code at typical fMRI lengths). Differentiable
through `data`, not through `mask` (a boolean dispatcher). It is the 1-D temporal counterpart of `Linear`;
for spectrum-preserving interpolation under the Power-2014 censoring protocol the signal unit offers
`lomb_scargle_interpolate` instead. We do not expand it further here - it belongs to the signal domain,
and this chapter mentions it only to place the spatial resamplers in the wider family.

## Further reading

- Thévenaz, P., Blu, T., and Unser, M. (2000). "Interpolation Revisited." *IEEE Transactions on Medical
  Imaging*, 19(7), 739–758. The reference treatment of interpolation quality for medical images; the
  B-spline interpolation property and why a prefilter is needed.
- Unser, M., Aldroubi, A., and Eden, M. (1991). "Fast B-Spline Transforms for Continuous Image
  Representation and Interpolation." *IEEE TPAMI*, 13(3), 277–285. The recursive prefilter and the cubic
  pole $\sqrt{3}-2$ implemented here.
- Unser, M. (1999). "Splines: A Perfect Fit for Signal and Image Processing." *IEEE Signal Processing
  Magazine*, 16(6), 22–38. A readable overview of the whole B-spline-interpolation machinery.
- Duchon, C. E. (1979). "Lanczos Filtering in One and Two Dimensions." *Journal of Applied Meteorology*,
  18(8), 1016–1022. The windowed-sinc (Lanczos) kernel and its ringing/sharpness trade-off.
- Dalca, A. V., Balakrishnan, G., Guttag, J., and Sabuncu, M. R. (2019). "Unsupervised Learning of
  Probabilistic Diffeomorphic Registration for Images and Surfaces." *Medical Image Analysis*, 57,
  226–236. The voxelmorph spatial transformer and velocity integration; the edge-replicate boundary
  convention for flow warps.
- Jaderberg, M., Simonyan, K., Zisserman, A., and Kavukcuoglu, K. (2015). "Spatial Transformer Networks."
  *NeurIPS 2015*. The differentiable bilinear sampler that made coordinate-driven gradients standard in
  deep models.
- Avants, B. B., Tustison, N. J., Song, G., et al. (2011). "A Reproducible Evaluation of ANTs Similarity
  Metric Performance in Brain Image Registration." *NeuroImage*, 54(3), 2033–2044. The ANTs lineage these
  kernels (LanczosWindowedSinc, MultiLabel) follow in behaviour.
- Power, J. D., Mitra, A., Laumann, T. O., et al. (2014). "Methods to Detect, Characterize, and Remove
  Motion Artifact in Resting State fMRI." *NeuroImage*, 84, 320–341. The motion-censoring protocol behind
  `linear_interpolate` / `lomb_scargle_interpolate`.

## See also

- The coordinate sources — `identity_grid`, `spatial_transform`, `spatial_transform_batched`, `resample`,
  `sample_at_points`, `integrate_velocity_field` —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/grid.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/grid.py).
- The kernel ADT — `Interpolator`, `Linear`, `NearestNeighbour`, `Lanczos`, `CubicBSpline`, `MultiLabel`,
  the boundary folds, and both gather engines —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/_interpolate.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/_interpolate.py).
- The Gaussian pyramid — `gaussian_pyramid`, `downsample`, `upsample` —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/pyramid.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/pyramid.py).
- The anti-alias smoother the pyramid composes —
  [`src/nitrix/smoothing/gaussian.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/smoothing/gaussian.py).
- The 1-D temporal cousin — `linear_interpolate` —
  [`src/nitrix/signal/interpolate.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/signal/interpolate.py).
- Neighbouring pages: the pull-back convention these samplers realise
  ([Foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-pullback)); how a parametrised transform produces the deformation
  grid ([Transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-fit)); the coarse-to-fine schedule the pyramid feeds
  ([Optimization](optimization.qmd#sec-coarse-fine)); the similarity metrics computed on the warped image
  ([Similarity](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd)); and the velocity integration built on `spatial_transform`
  ([Diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf)).
